title: Choose Region and create Budget
content:
Choose Region Use one Region for the whole project, for example:
ap-southeast-1 Using one Region makes it easier for Lambda to call S3, DynamoDB, API Gateway, Bedrock, Polly, and Transcribe without configuration drift.
Create AWS Budget Open Billing and Cost Management. Choose Budgets -> Create budget.
Select Cost budget.
Set a monthly budget, for example 10 USD.
Add email alerts at 50%, 80%, and 100%.521.png Cost-sensitive services Service Reason Amazon Bedrock CV analysis, question generation, and scoring invoke models. Amazon Transcribe Charged by audio duration. Amazon Polly Charged by number of synthesized characters. Amplify Hosting Charged by build, storage, and data transfer. CloudWatch Logs Long-retained or excessive logs can create cost.

title: Configure frontend build and env
content:
Local build cd frontend npm install npm run build If build succeeds, Vite creates:
frontend/dist Amplify environment variables Add the same VITE_... variables as .env, but callback/logout URLs must use the real deployed domain.
Example:
VITE_COGNITO_REDIRECT_URI=https://main.dxxxx.amplifyapp.com VITE_COGNITO_LOGOUT_URI=https://main.dxxxx.amplifyapp.com Note After changing environment variables in Amplify, redeploy so Vite rebuilds the app with the new values.

title: Create bucket for CV and audio
content:
Create bucket Open Amazon S3 -> Create bucket 2. Use a unique bucket name, for example:
talent-graph-ai-storage-huydat Choose the same Region as Lambda.
Enable Block all public access. Versioning can be enabled during testing.
Create the bucket.
Required environment variables STORAGE_BUCKET=talent-graph-ai-storage-huydat VOICE_BUCKET=talent-graph-ai-storage-huydat Checks Bucket is not public. Lambda role has s3:GetObject, s3:PutObject. After testing CV upload, an object appears under cv/.

title: Create routes and Lambda integrations
content: Create API Open Amazon API Gateway. Choose Create API. Choose HTTP API. Create integrations to each Lambda. Create routes from the table in section 5.7. Create a stage, for example: default Copy the invoke URL for the frontend. Example deployed endpoint https://<api-id>.execute-api.ap-southeast-1.amazonaws.com/default/upload_cv Route checks Each route points to the correct Lambda. Lambda permission allows API Gateway to invoke the function. Stage is deployed or auto deploy is enabled.

title: Create User Pool and App Client
content:
Create User Pool Open Amazon Cognito -> User pools. Choose Create user pool.
For Define your application options, choose Traditional web application.
Enable required attributes: email name phone_number Use email verification. Create the User Pool. Create App Client In the User Pool, open App clients. Choose Create app client. Select a web application client type. Do not enable client secret for a React SPA. Copy the App client ID for: VITE_COGNITO_CLIENT_ID=<app-client-id> Check User Pool ID looks like ap-southeast-1_xxxxx. App Client ID is saved. Email verification works for new users.

title: Create Users table
content: Create table Open DynamoDB -> Tables -> Create table. Table name: Users Partition key: userId Type: String. Billing mode: On-demand. Create the table. Sample item { "userId": "cognito-sub-id", "fullName": "Nguyen Huy Dat", "email": "user@example.com", "phone": "", "avatarUrl": "", "role": "user", "createdAt": "2026-07-21T00:00:00Z" } Lambdas using this table profile_api history_api admin_api

title: Deploy upload_cv, profile_api, history_api
content:
Deploy upload_cv Create Lambda upload_cv. Runtime: Python 3.12. Copy code from: backend/upload_cv/lambda_function.py Add environment variables: IAM needs s3:PutObject, dynamodb:PutItem. Deploy profile_api backend/profile_api/lambda_function.py Environment variable:
IAM needs dynamodb:GetItem, dynamodb:PutItem, dynamodb:UpdateItem.
Deploy history_api backend/history_api/lambda_function.py Environment variables:
IAM needs dynamodb:Query on CVs and Interviews.

title: Enable Amazon Bedrock model access
content: Enable model access Open Amazon Bedrock. Select the correct Region. Open Model access. Request or enable Nova Lite if available for the account. Wait until model access is granted. Environment variables BEDROCK_MODEL_ID=apac.amazon.nova-lite-v1:0 BEDROCK_REGION=ap-southeast-1 Lambdas using Bedrock analyze_cv create_interview submit_answer Quick test Call analyze_cv with an uploaded userId and cvId. If Bedrock is not ready, Lambda should use fallback logic so the app can still run during demo.

title: Overview
content:
Goal This project aims to build an intelligent interview training system based on personal CVs. The system uses AWS Serverless services combined with AI to analyze CVs, generate interview questions based on roles, support written or voice responses, evaluate answers, and record training history. Through this, users can improve their interview skills, identify strengths and weaknesses, and better prepare for the recruitment process.
Main features Feature Description Authentication Users sign up/sign in through Amazon Cognito Hosted UI. CV upload Frontend sends base64 CV to Lambda; Lambda stores the file in S3. CV analysis Lambda reads CV from S3 and analyzes it with Amazon Bedrock Nova Lite or fallback logic. AI Interview The system creates role/CV-based questions; question count is selectable from frontend. Scoring Lambda receives answers and returns score, feedback, and improvement advice. Voice Polly creates question audio; Transcribe converts answer audio to text. History/Result Users review CV history, interview history, final score, and advice. Admin Console Admins manage users, CVs, interviews, review queue, audit log, and CSV export. Integrated AWS services Amazon Cognito Amazon S3 Amazon DynamoDB AWS Lambda Amazon API Gateway Amazon Bedrock Amazon Polly Amazon Transcribe Amazon CloudWatch AWS Amplify Hosting Deployment flow Prepare Region, budget, source code, and environment variables. Create Cognito for user authentication. Create S3 and DynamoDB for storage/database. Deploy Lambda backend. Create API Gateway routes and JWT Authorizer. Enable Bedrock/Polly/Transcribe for AI and voice. Deploy frontend with Amplify Hosting. Test the full user flow and admin flow.

title: Test user flow
content: Checklist Step Action Expected result 1 Sign in with Cognito Returns to frontend and displays the correct user. 2 Upload CV S3 has the file, DynamoDB CVs has metadata. 3 Analyze CV CV has skills, summary, suggested role. 4 Create interview DynamoDB Interviews has questions. 5 Answer questions API returns score and feedback. 6 Complete Result shows final score and general feedback. 7 Open History CV/interview history exists and detail view works. If data appears as a new user Check userId from the Cognito token. If the old data used demo localStorage user_demo_001, but deployment uses Cognito sub, old data will not match automatically. Migrate data or use the matching userId in DynamoDB.

title: Event 1
content:
Reflection on the “GenAI-powered App-DB Modernization” Workshop Event Overview The workshop on May 30 focused on application and database modernization in the age of GenAI. Instead of only introducing technical tools, the event connected several important topics: systems thinking, DevOps culture, cloud learning, psychological barriers, and practical product-building through hackathon-style work.
The main message I received from the event was that building technology products is not only about choosing the right AWS service or automation tool. A successful project also depends on communication, ownership, feedback loops, and the ability to keep moving even when the first version is not perfect.
Speakers Tran Minh Quan - Intern Tran Huu Nghia - Intern The Baller - Intern Pham Quang Thai - Intern Nguyen Thi Quynh Nhu - Intern Khac Uy - Intern Huynh Thai Linh - Intern Main Topics Mindset, People, and Technology One point that stood out to me was the way the presentations combined technical knowledge with human factors. The event did not treat problems such as missed deadlines, production bugs, poor collaboration, or procrastination as isolated issues. Instead, these problems were explained as symptoms of deeper causes.
For example, in project work, visible problems may come from weak communication, separated teams, manual processes, or unclear responsibility. In personal development, procrastination may not simply come from laziness, but from fear of failure, fear of judgment, or lack of confidence.
DevOps Culture The DevOps section helped me understand that DevOps is not only a collection of tools. The core of DevOps is the balance between People, Process, and Technology.
Automation can improve deployment speed and reduce manual mistakes, but it cannot replace trust, communication, and accountability inside a team. This idea is important because many technical problems actually start from workflow and collaboration problems.
Cloud Learning and Cost Control The event also discussed how beginners can learn AWS more safely. A common concern when practicing cloud services is unexpected billing, especially when resources are not deleted after testing.
To reduce that risk, the speakers introduced learning methods such as AWS Cloud Quest and local simulation with Floci. Floci was presented as a lightweight open-source AWS emulator that can help test cloud architecture locally with less cost and faster feedback.
Practical Product Building Another valuable topic was the “build fast, fail fast” mindset. Through examples from hackathon environments, I saw how teams can move quickly by focusing on the core problem, building a usable version first, receiving feedback, and improving the product step by step.
The event mentioned practical ideas such as SynthHunter, which helps distinguish AI-generated voices from real human voices to reduce fraud risks, and Vortex, an application that supports the recruitment process. These examples showed that strong ideas usually come from solving real problems.
Technical Knowledge Gained Application Design and Modernization I learned more about how modern systems can be analyzed and redesigned through practices such as event storming. This approach helps convert business processes into domain events, making it easier to understand how different parts of a system interact.
The workshop also introduced concepts such as bounded contexts, microservice splitting, and communication patterns. I became more aware of the trade-offs between synchronous and asynchronous communication, as well as integration styles such as pub/sub, point-to-point messaging, and streaming.
GenAI in the Software Development Lifecycle The event introduced Amazon Q Developer as a tool that can support different stages of the software development lifecycle, from planning and coding to modernization and maintenance.
I also learned how AI-assisted tools can help with code transformation and how serverless services such as AWS Lambda can support faster experimentation and deployment.
Personal Takeaways Overcoming Psychological Barriers A meaningful lesson from the event was that 70-80% of procrastination can come from difficulty managing emotions rather than laziness. Fear of not being good enough, fear of being judged, and fear of failure can prevent people from starting work.
Two simple methods that I found practical were the 5-Minute Rule and the 5-Second Rule. The 5-Minute Rule encourages starting a difficult task for just five minutes to create momentum. The 5-Second Rule uses a short countdown to push the body into action before hesitation takes over.
Applying the Lessons to My Work After the workshop, I think there are several ideas I can apply to my own learning and project work:
Build stronger team workflows by encouraging shared responsibility and reducing blame. Create faster feedback loops so problems can be discovered early. Name my fears clearly instead of simply saying that I am lazy. Use small daily progress to build confidence. Practice AWS through safer methods such as AWS Cloud Quest and local simulation tools. Keep a “go build” mindset by making a usable version first, then improving it through feedback. Event Experience Overall, the workshop gave me a broader view of how technology, workflow, and psychology are connected. I not only gained technical knowledge about cloud modernization, microservices, GenAI tools, and serverless development, but also learned how mindset and team culture affect the success of a project.
The most important thing I took away is that good engineering is not only about writing code. It also requires clear communication, responsibility, experimentation, and the courage to start before everything feels perfect.
Event Photos Add event photos here.

title: Worklog
content:
This worklog records the internship progress and the development process of Vertex-IntervAI over 12 weeks, from 17 April 2026 to 12 July 2026.
The content is summarized from the internship progress report and focuses on learning AWS Cloud, researching generative AI, designing a serverless architecture, building backend/database modules, and completing the main workflows of the AI interview system.
Progress Overview Week Timeline Main Content Details Week 1 17 Apr 2026 - 26 Apr 2026 Internship orientation and aws cloud familiarization View details Week 2 27 Apr 2026 - 03 May 2026 Studying basic aws services View details Week 3 04 May 2026 - 10 May 2026 Project topic discussion and amazon bedrock research View details Week 4 11 May 2026 - 17 May 2026 Ai idea research and serverless architecture View details Week 5 18 May 2026 - 24 May 2026 Requirement survey and project topic alignment View details Week 6 25 May 2026 - 31 May 2026 Finalizing scope and initial architecture design View details Week 7 01 Jun 2026 - 07 Jun 2026 Backend initialization and database design View details Week 8 08 Jun 2026 - 14 Jun 2026 Building the cv upload feature View details Week 9 15 Jun 2026 - 21 Jun 2026 Developing ai interview sessions and answer storage View details Week 10 22 Jun 2026 - 28 Jun 2026 User profile api and activity history View details Week 11 29 Jun 2026 - 05 Jul 2026 Completing profile, cv history, and web optimization View details Week 12 06 Jul 2026 - 12 Jul 2026 Api testing and access permission fixes View details Overall Results Completed the serverless backend foundation for the main features of Vertex-IntervAI. Designed and used data tables for users, CVs, and interview sessions. Integrated storage, CV analysis, AI interview generation, answer scoring, and history/result review workflows. Tested APIs, fixed access permission issues, configured environments, and prepared the system for demo/deployment.

title: Event 2
content: Summary Report: 20/6 Event Overview The event features 8 participating teams, divided into 4 rounds. The first round consists of 4 matches, with 2 teams competing against each other, each choosing a set of questions from the program. In each match, the two teams will answer 10 questions with increasing difficulty. A correct answer earns 10 points, while an incorrect answer deducts 10 points. Each round will showcase interesting skills such as: double points for correct answers but double points for incorrect answers, and the ability to answer incorrectly without losing points… To increase the excitement and drama of each round, the program will gradually increase the difficulty of the questions, and points will be added or deducted accordingly. Connect and exchange While the teams are exchanging ideas, those below can volunteer to answer the question and give hints for their answer. Those who answer correctly will receive attractive prizes from the program. Event Experience After participating in the event, I was able to learn a lot about things I had never learned before and improve my listening skills. Some event photos

title: Configure Hosted UI and Groups
content: Configure Hosted UI Open the created App Client. Open Login pages, Hosted UI, or Managed login. Add callback URLs: http://localhost:5173 https://<your-amplify-domain> Add sign-out URLs: http://localhost:5173 https://<your-amplify-domain> Select OAuth scopes: openid email profile phone Create user/admin groups In the User Pool, open Groups. Create group user. Create group admin. Select the administrator account and add it to admin. Note After adding a user to the admin group, sign out and sign in again so the new token contains the group claim. The frontend should only show Admin Console for admins, while admin_api must still validate the token for security.

title: Configure JWT Authorizer and CORS
content:
Create JWT Authorizer In API Gateway, open Authorizers. Choose Create -> JWT. Issuer URL: https://cognito-idp.<region>.amazonaws.com/<user-pool-id> Audience: Cognito App Client ID. Attach authorizer to routes requiring sign-in. CORS Allowed origins:
http://localhost:5173 https://<your-amplify-domain> Allowed methods:
GET,POST,OPTIONS Allowed headers:
content-type,authorization Error checks 401: invalid token or missing authorizer config. 403: user lacks permission or admin group is incorrect. CORS error: browser blocks the request before Lambda is invoked.

title: Configure Polly and Transcribe
content:
Amazon Polly Polly is used for English questions:
POLLY_VOICE_ID=Joanna POLLY_ENGINE=standard polly_speech creates an MP3 file, stores it in S3, and returns a presigned URL for the frontend to play.
Amazon Transcribe Transcribe is used for microphone input:
TRANSCRIBE_LANGUAGE_CODE=en-US If the user selects Vietnamese, frontend/backend should send:
vi-VN Vietnamese note Amazon Transcribe supports Vietnamese speech recognition with vi-VN. For Vietnamese question speech, Amazon Polly does not provide a common Vietnamese voice like English voices, so browser speech synthesis or another Vietnamese TTS provider can be used.
Microphone checks The deployed frontend must use HTTPS. Audio must be fully uploaded to S3. Transcribe job should be polled until COMPLETED. If transcript is too short, check recording duration, silence at the start, and languageCode.

title: Configure S3 CORS and IAM
Create CVs and Interviews tables
content: Create CVs table Config Value Table name CVs Partition key userId Sort key cvId Billing mode On-demand Create Interviews table Config Value Table name Interviews Partition key userId Sort key interviewId Billing mode On-demand Data checks after running the app After CV upload, an item must exist in CVs. After CV analysis, the CVs item should include skills, summary, status. After creating an interview, an item must exist in Interviews. After answering questions, Interviews should include answers, attempts, score, or finalScore. Lambda environment variables CVS_TABLE=CVs INTERVIEWS_TABLE=Interviews

Deploy analyze_cv Create Lambda analyze_cv. Copy code from: backend/analyze_cv/lambda_function.py Add environment variables: Add PDF extraction layer if needed: backend/analyze_cv/analyze_cv_pypdf_layer.zip Deploy create_interview backend/create_interview/lambda_function.py Environment variables: Deploy submit_answer backend/submit_answer/lambda_function.py Environment variables:
These Lambdas need bedrock:InvokeModel and matching DynamoDB read/write permissions.

Frontend environment variables frontend/.env needs API endpoints and Cognito config:
Local check cd frontend npm install npm run dev Can run http://localhost:5173 to verify the UI before deploying to Amplify.

title: Proposal
content:
Vertex-IntervAI Proposal AWS Serverless AI Interview Training Platform 1. Executive Summary Vertex-IntervAI is an AI-powered interview training platform that helps students and job seekers prepare for recruitment more effectively. Instead of using generic question lists, the system starts from the user’s own CV, analyzes their skills and experience, generates interview questions for a selected role, evaluates answers, and returns feedback with scores and improvement suggestions.
The proposed solution uses a React + Vite frontend and an AWS Serverless backend. Core services include Amazon Cognito for authentication, Amazon S3 for CV and audio storage, Amazon DynamoDB for structured data, AWS Lambda and Amazon API Gateway for backend APIs, Amazon Bedrock for CV analysis and interview intelligence, Amazon Polly for question audio, Amazon Transcribe for speech-to-text, Amazon CloudWatch for logs, and AWS Amplify Hosting for frontend deployment.
2. Problem Statement Current Problem Many students and early-career candidates prepare for interviews without personalized feedback. Public question banks are easy to find, but they do not adapt to the candidate’s real CV, project experience, skill level, or target job role. Manual mock interviews also require mentors, time, and repeated scheduling, which makes frequent practice difficult.
Proposed Solution Vertex-IntervAI provides a self-service interview preparation workflow:
Users sign up and sign in through Amazon Cognito. Users upload one or more CV files. The backend stores CV files securely in Amazon S3. The system analyzes CV content and extracts skills, education, projects, experience, and role suggestions. Users choose a target role and number of questions. The system generates interview questions based on the CV and selected role. Users answer by text or microphone. The backend evaluates answers and returns score, feedback, strengths, weaknesses, and advice. Users can review their interview history and results later. Admin users can monitor users, CVs, interviews, review queues, audit logs, and CSV exports. 3. Project Objectives The project aims to deliver a practical AI interview training system with the following goals:
Build a complete web application for CV upload, AI interview practice, result review, and profile management. Apply AWS Serverless architecture to reduce infrastructure management and keep the system scalable. Use Amazon Bedrock to support CV analysis, question generation, and answer evaluation. Support voice-based interview practice with Amazon Polly and Amazon Transcribe. Store user data, CV metadata, interview sessions, and results in a structured and reusable way. Provide an admin console for monitoring and operational management. Prepare a deployment workshop that can be followed step by step on AWS. 4. Solution Architecture The architecture separates the frontend, authentication layer, API layer, storage layer, AI services, and monitoring layer. The frontend communicates with API Gateway endpoints and passes Cognito JWT tokens for protected operations. API Gateway invokes Lambda functions, which coordinate with S3, DynamoDB, Bedrock, Polly, and Transcribe.
AWS Services Used Service Purpose Amazon Cognito Sign-up, sign-in, Hosted UI, JWT authentication, and user/admin authorization. Amazon S3 Store uploaded CV files, generated audio, answer recordings, and transcript artifacts. Amazon DynamoDB Store users, CV metadata, interview sessions, answers, scores, and history. AWS Lambda Run backend functions for upload, CV analysis, profile, interview creation, answer scoring, voice, history, and admin operations. Amazon API Gateway Expose REST API endpoints and protect routes with JWT authorizers and CORS configuration. Amazon Bedrock Analyze CVs, generate interview questions, evaluate answers, and create improvement advice. Amazon Polly Convert interview questions into audio. Amazon Transcribe Convert candidate voice answers into text for evaluation. Amazon CloudWatch Store logs and support debugging for Lambda and API Gateway. AWS Amplify Hosting Build and deploy the React + Vite frontend. 5. Component Design Component Responsibility Frontend Provides login, dashboard, CV upload, interview, result, history, profile, settings, and admin pages. Authentication Uses Cognito Hosted UI and app client configuration to manage user sessions. CV pipeline Uploads CV files to S3, stores metadata in DynamoDB, and analyzes content through Lambda and Bedrock. Interview engine Generates questions, receives answers, scores responses, and stores results. Voice module Uses Polly for question audio and Transcribe for answer transcription. Admin module Allows administrators to review users, CVs, interviews, audit logs, and exported data. Monitoring Uses CloudWatch logs to trace API errors and backend behavior. 6. Technical Implementation Plan The workshop implementation is divided into service-based stages:
Prepare AWS Region, budget alert, source code, and environment variables. Create Cognito User Pool, App Client, Hosted UI domain, callback URLs, logout URLs, and user/admin groups. Create S3 buckets for CV and audio storage with CORS and IAM permissions. Create DynamoDB tables for Users, CVs, and Interviews. Deploy Lambda functions for CV, profile, history, interview, voice, and admin features. Create API Gateway routes, connect Lambda integrations, configure CORS, and add JWT authorization. Enable Amazon Bedrock model access and connect Bedrock to the AI-related Lambda functions. Configure Amazon Polly and Amazon Transcribe for voice support. Configure frontend .env variables and deploy the React + Vite app with Amplify Hosting. Test the user flow, admin flow, voice flow, result page, and history page. Clean up resources after the workshop to avoid unnecessary cost. 7. Timeline and Milestones The implementation worklog covers 6 working weeks. In the overall project timeline, Worklog Week 1 starts at Project Week 7, after the initial research and proposal preparation phase.
Project week Worklog week Main focus Milestones Week 7 Project initialization and architecture design Analyze requirements, create the React project, build basic routing and page layout, design DynamoDB schemas, prepare S3 storage, define the AI workflow, and draft the AWS architecture checklist. Week 8 Authentication, CV upload, and storage Complete Login UI, integrate Cognito Hosted UI, build drag-and-drop CV upload, create upload_cv Lambda, store files in S3, save CV metadata in DynamoDB, and configure callback/logout URLs. Week 9 CV analysis and dashboard Build Dashboard data views, create analyze_cv Lambda, read CV files from S3, analyze CV content with Bedrock/Nova Lite, store extracted skills/projects/experience, and protect APIs with Cognito JWT authorizer. Week 10 AI interview and answer scoring Build the AI Interview page, create interview sessions, generate questions from CV and role, save answers, score responses, return feedback and suggested answers, and calculate the final interview score. Week 11 Voice, history, profile, settings, and admin Add History, Profile, and Settings pages, implement history_api and profile_api, integrate Polly and Transcribe for voice, build Admin Console, export CSV data, and prepare feedback email workflow. Week 12 Testing, deployment, documentation, and demo Polish UI, test responsive layout, verify API Gateway/Lambda/DynamoDB/IAM/CORS, improve AI prompts and scoring, prepare deployment guide, capture screenshots, finalize worklog, and complete the demo checklist. 8. Budget Estimation For a student workshop and low-traffic demo, the system is designed to stay lightweight. Most services can remain low cost when the number of users, uploaded files, and AI calls are controlled.
Cost Area Notes Lambda and API Gateway Charged by request count and execution time; expected to be low for demo traffic. S3 Charged by storage size and requests; CV and audio files should be cleaned up after testing. DynamoDB On-demand usage is suitable for small demo workloads. Bedrock Main variable cost; depends on number of CV analyses, generated questions, and answer evaluations. Polly and Transcribe Depends on amount of generated audio and uploaded answer recordings. Amplify Hosting Depends on build minutes, storage, and bandwidth. Cost control actions:
Set an AWS Budget alert before deployment. Use a single AWS Region. Limit test users and uploaded file sizes. Delete test CVs, audio files, and unused resources after the workshop. Stop using Bedrock/Transcribe tests once the demo is complete. 9. Risk Assessment Risk Impact Mitigation Incorrect Cognito callback/logout URL Users cannot sign in or return to the frontend. Verify local and Amplify URLs before testing. API Gateway CORS or JWT misconfiguration Frontend cannot call backend APIs. Test each route with browser dev tools and CloudWatch logs. Bedrock model access unavailable AI analysis or scoring cannot run. Enable model access early and keep fallback logic for demo mode. Large CV/audio files increase cost or fail upload Higher cost and unstable demo behavior. Add file size limits and clean up S3 test data. Voice permissions blocked by browser Microphone flow may fail during demo. Keep text-answer flow as a reliable backup. 10. Expected Outcomes After completion, Vertex-IntervAI should provide a working AI interview preparation workflow where users can sign in, upload CVs, receive AI analysis, practice interviews, answer by text or voice, view scores and feedback, and review history. The workshop also produces reusable documentation for deploying the same system on AWS Serverless infrastructure.

title: Test admin, voice, and logs
content:
Admin flow Admin user must belong to group admin. Normal users cannot see Admin Console. Admin can view users, CVs, and interviews. Block/delete user, delete interview, and export CSV work correctly. Voice flow Website runs on HTTPS for microphone access. English questions can play audio. A 30-60 second recording returns the main transcript content. Switching language does not reset current questions. CloudWatch Logs Check these log groups:
/aws/lambda/upload_cv /aws/lambda/analyze_cv /aws/lambda/create_interview /aws/lambda/submit_answer /aws/lambda/transcribe_audio /aws/lambda/admin_api If API returns 500, check Lambda logs first. If CORS or 401/403 occurs, check API Gateway and Cognito authorizer.

title: Week 2 Worklog
content:
Week 2 Objectives: Studying basic aws services Understand the role of foundational AWS services used in the project. Complete guided hands-on labs to learn resource deployment workflows. Record the services that can support the AI interview system. Tasks to be carried out this week: Day Task Start Date Completion Date Reference Material 1 - Studied S3 bucket, object, and policy organization. 27/04/2026 27/04/2026 Amazon S3 Documentation 2 - Studied DynamoDB, partition keys, sort keys, and NoSQL data modeling. 28/04/2026 29/04/2026 DynamoDB Documentation 3 - Practiced basic AWS Lambda and checked execution logs in CloudWatch. 30/04/2026 30/04/2026 AWS Lambda Workshop 4 - Completed an API Gateway lab connected to Lambda.
- Recorded how frontend clients call API endpoints. 01/05/2026 01/05/2026 API Gateway Documentation 5 - Summarized the learned services and mapped them to project requirements. 02/05/2026 02/05/2026 FCJ Workshop Week 2 Achievements: Understood the roles of S3, DynamoDB, Lambda, API Gateway, and CloudWatch in serverless architecture. Completed the basic guided labs. Identified suitable services for storage, API, and backend processing in the project.

title: Deploy Amazon Cognito
content:
Goal Cognito provides sign-up, sign-in, JWT authentication, and user/admin authorization for Vertex-IntervAI.
Components to create Component Role User Pool Stores user accounts. App Client Allows the React frontend to use Cognito Hosted UI. Hosted UI Cognito-managed sign-in/sign-up pages. Groups Provides user and admin roles. Callback URL Frontend URL after successful sign-in. After completion, the frontend can redirect users to Hosted UI and receive tokens after sign-in.

title: Deploy voice and admin Lambdas
content:
Deploy polly_speech backend/polly_speech/lambda_function.py Environment variables:
IAM needs polly:SynthesizeSpeech, s3:PutObject.
Deploy transcribe_audio backend/transcribe_audio/lambda_function.py Environment variables:
IAM needs transcribe:StartTranscriptionJob, transcribe:GetTranscriptionJob, s3:PutObject, s3:GetObject.
Deploy admin_api backend/admin_api/lambda_function.py Admin API needs matching read/write permissions on Users, CVs, and Interviews. If it blocks users with Cognito, the Lambda role also needs permissions such as cognito-idp:AdminDisableUser and cognito-idp:AdminEnableUser.

content:
Week 3 Objectives: Project topic discussion and amazon bedrock research Discuss AI application ideas for interviews and recruitment support. Study Amazon Bedrock and generative AI model capabilities. title: Blogs Posted
content:
This section will list and introduce the blogs you have posted to AWS Study Group. For example:
Blog 1 - SESSION POLICIES IN AMAZON EKS POD IDENTITY This blog introduces the newly added session policies feature in Amazon EKS Pod Identity, which allows you to narrow IAM permissions flexibly and precisely for each pod without needing to create multiple separate IAM roles. This is an important step forward that helps apply the principle of least privilege more effectively in large-scale Kubernetes environments.
Blog 2 - … This blog introduces the newly added session policies feature in Amazon EKS Pod Identity, which allows you to narrow IAM permissions flexibly and precisely for each pod without needing to create multiple separate IAM roles. This is an important step forward that helps apply the principle of least privilege more effectively in large-scale Kubernetes environments.
Blog 3 - … This blog introduces the newly added session policies feature in Amazon EKS Pod Identity, which allows you to narrow IAM permissions flexibly and precisely for each pod without needing to create multiple separate IAM roles. This is an important step forward that helps apply the principle of least privilege more effectively in large-scale Kubernetes environments.

title: Deploy Amazon S3
content:
Goal Amazon S3 stores files that should not be stored directly in the database: CVs, question audio, answer audio, and transcripts.
Object structure cv/{userId}/{cvId}.{extension} voice/questions/{userId}/{interviewId}/{questionIndex}.mp3 voice/answers/{userId}/{interviewId}/{questionIndex}.webm voice/transcripts/{jobName}.json The bucket is used by upload_cv, analyze_cv, polly_speech, transcribe_audio, and several admin features.

- Recorded the advantages of serverless for a student project. 13/05/2026 14/05/2026 AWS Serverless Documentation 4 - Drafted backend modules: upload CV, analyze CV, profile, interview, and voice. 15/05/2026 15/05/2026 Project Architecture Notes 5 - Discussed with the team and agreed on the next implementation direction. 16/05/2026 16/05/2026 Team Meeting Notes Week 4 Achievements: Selected the direction of building an AI interview system based on CV data. Understood how to organize the backend with serverless architecture. Completed the list of main modules required for the project.

Event name Date and time Location (if applicable) Your role in the event (attendee, event support, speaker, etc.) A brief description of the event’s content and main activities Outcomes or value gained (lessons learned, new skills, contribution to the team/project) This listing helps demonstrate your actual participation as well as the soft skills and experience you have gained from each event. title: Deploy Amazon DynamoDB
content:
Goal DynamoDB is the main database of the system, storing user profiles, CV metadata, and interview data.
Tables to deploy Table Partition key Sort key Content Users userId None Profile, fullname, email, phone, avatar, role. CVs userId cvId File metadata, status, skills, summary, suggested role. Interviews userId interviewId Questions, answers, score, feedback, status, role.

title: Workshop
content:
Vertex-IntervAI Deployment Workshop This workshop explains how to deploy Vertex-IntervAI on AWS by service. The structure follows a real deployment workshop: prepare the environment, deploy each AWS service, connect the frontend, test the system, and clean up resources.
Content Overview Environment preparation Deploy Amazon Cognito Deploy Amazon S3 Deploy Amazon DynamoDB Deploy AWS Lambda Deploy Amazon API Gateway Deploy AI and Voice services Deploy Frontend System testing Clean up resources Video Demo Expected result After the workshop, users can access the real website, sign in with Cognito, upload CVs, analyze CVs with AI, create interview sessions, answer questions with text/microphone, review results, and manage data through the Admin Console.

title: Deploy AWS Lambda
content:
Goal Deploy backend Lambdas from the backend/ folder. Each Lambda handles one feature group for easier debugging and scaling.
Lambda list Lambda Main route Role upload_cv POST /upload_cv Upload CV to S3 and save metadata. analyze_cv POST /analyze_cv Analyze CV using Bedrock/fallback. profile_api GET/POST /profile Read/write user profile. history_api GET /history Read CV and interview history. create_interview POST /interviews Create interview questions. submit_answer POST /interviews/answer Score answers. polly_speech POST /voice/question-audio Create question audio. transcribe_audio POST /voice/transcribe Convert audio to text. admin_api /admin/* Manage admin data.

content:
Week 6 Objectives: Finalizing scope and initial architecture design Finalize the project idea, feature scope, and team task allocation. Design the overall architecture between React frontend and AWS Serverless backend. Define stored data for Users, CVs, and Interviews. Tasks to be carried out this week: Day Task Start Date Completion Date Reference Material 1 - Finalized feature scope: CV upload, CV analysis, AI interview, results, and history. 25/05/2026 25/05/2026 Project Scope Notes 2 - Assigned team tasks and took responsibility for Backend and Database. 26/05/2026 26/05/2026 Team Planning 3 - Designed the initial architecture using React, API Gateway, Lambda, DynamoDB, S3, and Bedrock.
- Defined the main request flows. 27/05/2026 28/05/2026 Architecture Diagram 4 - Drafted the Users, CVs, and Interviews tables. 29/05/2026 29/05/2026 DynamoDB Documentation 5 - Prepared the backend Lambda folder structure and environment variable list. 30/05/2026 30/05/2026 Project Repository Week 6 Achievements: Completed an implementation scope suitable for the internship timeline. Prepared the initial architecture for Vertex-IntervAI. Clearly identified the required Backend and Database work.

title: Self-Assessment
content:
During my internship at Amazon Web Services Vietnam Company Limited from 04/17/2026 to 07/30/2026, I learned and developed many aspects of myself, gaining more confidence and improving my professional skills.
I participated in building a web application system on the AWS platform, with tasks related to backend and database management, and learned about and using AWS services. Through this, I enhanced my knowledge of cloud computing, CI/CD development processes, and improved my system design, problem-solving, self-learning, and teamwork skills.
Regarding work ethic, I always strived to complete tasks well, adhered to regulations, and actively exchanged ideas with colleagues to improve work efficiency.
To objectively reflect on my performance, I would like to self-assess based on the following criteria:
No. Criteria Description Good Fair Average 1 Professional knowledge & skills Understanding of the field, applying knowledge in practice, proficiency with tools, work quality ✅ ☐ ☐ 2 Ability to learn Ability to absorb new knowledge and learn quickly ✅ ✅ ☐ 3 Proactiveness Taking initiative, seeking out tasks without waiting for instructions ✅ ☐ ☐ 4 Sense of responsibility Completing tasks on time and ensuring quality ☐ ✅ ☐ 5 Discipline Adhering to schedules, rules, and work processes ☐ ✅ ☐ 6 Progressive mindset Willingness to receive feedback and improve oneself ☐ ✅ ☐ 7 Communication Presenting ideas and reporting work clearly ☐ ✅ ☐ 8 Teamwork Working effectively with colleagues and participating in teams ✅ ☐ ☐ 9 Professional conduct Respecting colleagues, partners, and the work environment ✅ ☐ ☐ 10 Problem-solving skills Identifying problems, proposing solutions, and showing creativity ☐ ✅ ☐ 11 Contribution to project/team Work effectiveness, innovative ideas, recognition from the team ✅ ☐ ☐ 12 Overall General evaluation of the entire internship period ✅ ☐ ☐ Needs Improvement Gain deeper insights into advanced AWS services and modern deployment models to expand your expertise. Enhance discipline and strict adherence to company or organizational regulations. Develop problem-solving skills and teamwork abilities. Learn to communicate effectively in daily interactions and at work, and to handle situations more efficiently.

title: Deploy Amazon API Gateway
content:
Goal API Gateway creates HTTPS endpoints for the frontend to call backend Lambdas. It connects the React app with AWS-side processing services.
Routes overview Method Route Lambda POST /upload_cv upload_cv POST /analyze_cv analyze_cv GET/POST /profile profile_api GET /history history_api POST /interviews create_interview POST /interviews/answer submit_answer POST /voice/question-audio polly_speech POST /voice/transcribe transcribe_audio ANY /admin/{proxy+} admin_api

- Tested S3 access permissions from Lambda. 04/06/2026 05/06/2026 Amazon S3 Documentation 5 - Documented required environment variables and IAM permissions for the backend. 06/06/2026 06/06/2026 IAM Documentation Week 7 Achievements: Completed the initial backend structure. Created a data model for CVs, user profiles, and interviews. Connected Lambda with S3 to prepare for the CV upload feature.

title: Sharing and Feedback
content:
Overall Feedback 1. Working environment
The working environment is friendly, open, and supportive. I felt comfortable asking questions and discussing problems with the team. The workspace was also organized and easy to work in, which helped me stay focused. One thing that could make the experience even better is having more team bonding activities so interns and team members can connect more naturally.
2. Support from mentor / team admin
My mentor gave clear guidance and explained things patiently whenever I had trouble understanding a task. I also appreciated being encouraged to try solving problems on my own before receiving the final answer. The admin team supported the internship process well and helped with the necessary documents and coordination.
3. Connection to my major
The assigned tasks were related to what I studied at university, but they also introduced me to new practical topics. This helped me connect theory with real work and made the learning process more meaningful.
4. Learning and skill development
During the internship, I improved several skills such as teamwork, communication, task tracking, and using project-related tools. I also learned from the experience shared by the mentor and team members, which gave me a clearer view of how real projects are handled in a company environment.
5. Company culture and teamwork
The company culture felt positive and respectful. Everyone worked seriously, but the atmosphere was still friendly and supportive. I noticed that when tasks became urgent, people were willing to help each other and keep the work moving forward together.
6. Internship support and benefits
The internship allowance and flexible support were helpful. I also value the opportunity to join internal activities and training sessions, because they gave me a better understanding of the company and helped me learn beyond daily tasks.
Additional Questions What did you enjoy most during the internship?
The part I enjoyed most was the opportunity to work on practical tasks and learn directly from real project experience. I also liked the supportive atmosphere because it made it easier for me to ask questions, receive feedback, and improve step by step.
What should the company improve for future interns?
The company could provide a clearer roadmap at the beginning of the internship, including expected milestones and suggested learning resources. This would help interns understand what they should focus on and track their progress more easily.
Would you recommend this internship to others? Why?
Yes, I would recommend this internship to other students. The program provides a good environment to learn cloud computing, improve teamwork skills, and experience how a real project is developed in a professional setting.
Suggestions Continue creating a supportive environment where interns can ask questions and learn by doing. Final Note Overall, I am grateful for the opportunity to join the program. It helped me gain practical experience, improve my confidence, and better understand professional working methods in a real project environment.

title: Deploy AI and Voice services
content:
Goal Enable AI/voice services so the system can analyze CVs, generate/score interview questions, and support microphone input.
Services used Service Role Amazon Bedrock Analyze CVs, generate questions, score answers. Amazon Polly Create interview question audio. Amazon Transcribe Convert answer audio to text. Amazon S3 Store audio and transcripts.

content:
Week 8 Objectives: Building the cv upload feature Build the upload_cv Lambda to receive base64 CV files and store them in S3. Validate PDF, DOC, and DOCX formats and save metadata to DynamoDB. Create the Upload CV API and test it with Postman/frontend. Tasks to be carried out this week: Day Task Start Date Completion Date Reference Material 1 - Built the upload_cv Lambda to process base64 files from the frontend. 08/06/2026 08/06/2026 AWS Lambda Documentation 2 - Added validation for PDF, DOC, DOCX formats and file size. 09/06/2026 09/06/2026 Project Validation Notes 3 - Stored CV files in S3 using cv/{userId}/{cvId}.{extension}. 10/06/2026 10/06/2026 Amazon S3 Documentation 4 - Saved CV metadata to the CVs DynamoDB table.
- Added uploaded/analyzed statuses for CV records. 11/06/2026 12/06/2026 DynamoDB Documentation 5 - Created the API Gateway route and tested CV upload with Postman. 13/06/2026 13/06/2026 Postman Collection Week 8 Achievements: Completed the CV upload API from frontend to AWS. CV files were stored in the correct S3 structure and metadata was saved to DynamoDB. Found and fixed several upload data issues during testing.

title: Deploy Frontend
content:
Goal Deploy the React + Vite frontend so other people can access a real HTTPS endpoint.
Suggested hosting options The frontend is a static React app, so it can be deployed with:
AWS Amplify Hosting Amazon S3 + CloudFront This workshop uses Amplify Hosting because it is quick, connects easily to GitHub, and can build from the frontend/ folder.

content:
Week 9 Objectives: Developing ai interview sessions and answer storage Build the AI interview session flow based on analyzed CV data. Create an API to receive answers, score them, and store results. Design interview history data for the History and Result pages. Tasks to be carried out this week: Day Task Start Date Completion Date Reference Material 1 - Built analyze_cv Lambda to read CV files from S3 and invoke Amazon Bedrock. 15/06/2026 15/06/2026 Amazon Bedrock Documentation 2 - Built create_interview Lambda to generate interview question sets. 16/06/2026 16/06/2026 Project Backend Notes 3 - Designed storage for questions, answers, attempts, and score in the Interviews table.
- Prepared data for the AI Interview screen. 17/06/2026 18/06/2026 DynamoDB Documentation 4 - Built submit_answer Lambda to score answers and save feedback. 19/06/2026 19/06/2026 AWS Lambda Documentation 5 - Tested the upload CV, analyze CV, create interview, and submit answer flow. 20/06/2026 20/06/2026 Postman Collection Week 9 Achievements: Created AI interview sessions based on CV data. The answer scoring API saved answers, attempts, and scores. Completed the data foundation for History and Result pages.

title: System testing
content:
Goal Run end-to-end tests to ensure the frontend, backend, and AWS services work together.
Test groups Group Content User flow Login, Upload CV, Analyze CV, AI Interview, Result, History. Voice flow Polly audio, microphone recording, Transcribe transcript. Admin flow Users, CVs, Interviews, Review Queue, Audit Log, Export CSV. Operation CloudWatch Logs, CORS, IAM, Budget.

Fix integration issues between frontend and backend in the main flows. Tasks to be carried out this week: Day Task Start Date Completion Date Reference Material 1 - Built profile_api Lambda with GET/POST profile operations in the Users table. 22/06/2026 22/06/2026 DynamoDB Documentation 2 - Added activity/history data for the dashboard and history pages. 23/06/2026 23/06/2026 Project Data Notes 3 - Checked answer, feedback, score, and final result storage.
- Fixed missing data issues in Interview records. 24/06/2026 24/06/2026 Project Backend Notes 4 - Connected frontend profileApi and interviewApi services to real endpoints. 25/06/2026 26/06/2026 Frontend Source Code 5 - Retested the user flow from CV upload to result review. 27/06/2026 27/06/2026 Manual Test Checklist Week 10 Achievements: Completed the basic user profile API. Answer and evaluation data became more complete in Interview records. Frontend started using backend data for dashboard, history, and result pages.

title: Clean up resources
content:
Goal Delete or disable unused resources after the demo to avoid unnecessary cost.
Cleanup checklist Resource Action Amplify app Delete the app if the public website is no longer needed. API Gateway Delete unused test stages/APIs. Lambda Delete test functions, unused versions/layers. S3 Delete test CV/audio/transcript objects; keep the bucket if demo continues. DynamoDB Export needed data before deleting tables. Cognito Delete test users, keep demo/admin users if needed. CloudWatch Logs Reduce retention or delete test log groups. Final check Open Billing dashboard to review cost. Check whether S3 still has large test files. Check whether Lambda/API Gateway still has test resources. Save endpoints, demo images, and key configuration for the report.

title: Week 11 Worklog
content:
Week 11 Objectives: Completing profile, cv history, and web optimization Complete personal information update APIs and CV/interview history retrieval. Optimize frontend speed and experience when handling multiple records. Fix synchronization issues between localStorage fallback and AWS data. Tasks to be carried out this week: Day Task Start Date Completion Date Reference Material 1 - Completed POST profile for updating fullname, email, phone, and avatar. 29/06/2026 29/06/2026 Profile API Notes 2 - Built the flow to retrieve CV and interview lists from DynamoDB. 30/06/2026 30/06/2026 DynamoDB Query Documentation 3 - Updated the dashboard to display multiple CVs, select a CV, and show matching details. 01/07/2026 01/07/2026 Frontend Source Code 4 - Optimized frontend services and localStorage fallback handling.
- Checked data synchronization across pages. 02/07/2026 03/07/2026 React/Vite Documentation 5 - Checked performance and fixed responsive UI issues. 04/07/2026 04/07/2026 Manual Test Checklist Week 11 Achievements: User profiles can be updated and displayed more accurately. Dashboard, Upload CV, History, and Result pages use more consistent data. Improved speed and UI behavior when the system contains many CV/interview records.
